narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated an 80.5% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger projected outcomes. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group and strongly signaled a higher risk of bar exam struggles compared to those who typically pass. Your Year 1 decile ranking, also at the lowest level, reinforced this concern by similarly pushing the prediction toward failure, far below the mid-to-upper ranges seen in successful bar takers. Interestingly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 actually worked in your favor, nudging the outlook slightly toward passing since it aligns closer to the averages of 3.24 among those who succeed, though it wasn't enough to offset the other factors. While your upper-middle family income and full-time study plans are common among passers—matching what most admitted students share—they didn't carry enough weight to change the overall picture, especially with your LSAT score of 31 lagging well behind the 37.27 average of bar passers. In essence, your early academic deciles stood out as the dominant drivers pulling the prediction downward, unlike the profiles of accepted students who generally show stronger year-end rankings. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag on bar exam patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking targeted academic advising to improve your class standings. Many students in similar positions have rebounded by addressing these exact areas before reapplying. With focused effort, you can shift your profile toward the successful averages and resubmit a compelling application next cycle.
